governing_equations: J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}  \text{ (Fick's first law: flux is proportional to gradient)}

socratic_plan.question: Draw on the canvas where you think the blue concentration will be after some time. Will it stay on the left, spread evenly, or something else?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student externalises their mental model of concentration spreading before observing it

socratic_plan.question: Click on where you think diffusion is happening the fastest right now.
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student identifies that the steepest concentration gradient drives the highest flux
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student clicks near the membrane / gradient zone

socratic_plan.question: What happens to how quickly the concentrations equalise when you increase D? Try it.
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student discovers that D directly controls the rate of spreading — it is the diffusion coefficient
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student observes and describes faster equilibration at higher D

socratic_plan.question: Now keep D the same and change the initial concentration. Does that change how fast the two sides equalise?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student discovers that the rate (time to equalise) is independent of the initial concentration for linear diffusion
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student notices the equalisation speed is unchanged

socratic_plan.question: Why does net diffusion stop once the concentrations on both sides are equal, even though molecules are still moving?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student connects the cessation of net flux to the zero concentration gradient at equilibrium
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student articulates that equal concentrations mean zero gradient and therefore zero net flux

verification.summary: Higher D reaches near-equilibrium faster; time to equilibrium is independent of initial concentration for linear diffusion.

probes.description: Low diffusion coefficient — slow approach to equilibrium
High diffusion coefficient — fast approach to equilibrium

invariants.description: Higher D should reach near-equilibrium in less wall time.
Time to near-equilibrium should be the same regardless of initial concentration (same D).
